source_locator.quote_or_paraphrase: Sea continental shelf off the north central Turkish seaport of Sinop using a side-scan sonar, small remotely operated vehicles, and a series of dredge lowerings located, inspected and sampled an exposed high-energy paleoshoreline at a depth of 155 m. Radiocarbon dating of mollusk shells collected from this ancient beach revealed that the marine flooding of the Black Sea took place between 7460 and 6820 yr B.P. (all ages are reported in radiocarbon years, not calendar years, without correction for reservoir age or dendro-calibration) changing it from a lacustrine to marine environment. This relic surface remained in contact with the bottom waters of the Black Sea for a long period of time before being draped by a thin layer of sapropel mud. source_locator.quote_or_paraphrase: These survey lines clearly delineated an ancient shoreline (Fig. 3) at a depth of 155 m. The lines were characterized by a typical beach profile complete with a beach berm, low-tide terrace, a longshore trough exposing the ancient lake bottom surface and a longshore sandbar. The survey lines ranged in depth from less than 100 m to greater than 180 m and nowhere else along these lines were these ancient shoreline features seen. This suggests that the drowning of this paleoshoreline was sudden and continued to the present level without the formation of other transgressional shoreline features typically found on other continental shelves in response to post-glacial sea-level fluctuations of the Holocene (Emery and Uchupi, 1984 and references therein).

source_locator.quote_or_paraphrase: Following the ROV operations, a series of frame dredge lowerings was made running parallel to contour across each of the paleoshoreline features ranging in water depth from 140 to 170 m. Inland from the ancient shoreline, a fine-grain sapropel mud was recovered containing well-preserved and blackened pieces of tree branches and leaves. Mixed in this mud were fresh marine mussel shells typical of those harvested by the local fisherman. Dredge hauls made along the ancient beach recovered large quantities of well-rounded beach pebbles of varied lithology (Fig. 4A) mixed within the sapropel mud. source_locator.quote_or_paraphrase: The more frequent sea-level stands broadly match the depth ranges of palaeoshorelines that have been mapped on the Australian continental shelf (Fig. 3). Given the stability of the shelf, its wide range of oceanographic regimes and high potential to preserve drowned structures, the results are useful as a global indicator of the depth zones in which shoreline structures were well-developed during the Late Quaternary. The sea-level modes identified indicate the range of depths in which palaeoshorelines, if preserved, are likely to occur on other shelves. For example, early Holocene coral reefs sit in depths of around 30–40 m at many locations on th

source_locator.quote_or_paraphrase: On the shelf edge seawards of the Great Barrier Reef (GBR), a series of coast-parallel banks and terraces are drowned reefs that last grew when sea level was tens of metres lower than present, and align with a number of sea-level modes (Table 1; Fig. 4e). Extensive carbonate banks on the middle to outer shelf, at a depth of approximately 27 m, appear to represent drowned coral reefs (Harris et al., 2013; Abbey et al., 2011). On the GBR shelf edge, terraces also occur at depths of approximately 40–50 m, 80–90 m and between 100 and 120 m (Beaman et al., 2008; Yokoyama et al., 2011), and record phases of reef growth that occurred from shortly before the Last Glacial Maxim

source_locator.quote_or_paraphrase: Evidence for erosion is observed across a range of modal depths on the Australian shelf, indicating repeated episodes of shoreline sculpturing during the Late Quaternary. Thus, benches cut into the seabed on the Rottnest and Lord Howe Island shelves occur at around the 70 m isobath (Brooke et al., 2010a, b). However, on the Lord Howe Island Shelf, there is a series of benches between 65 and 100 m depth (Woodroffe et al., 2010) that likely relate to interstadial periods of lower sea level, possibly in MIS 3. source_locator.quote_or_paraphrase: Wave-cut terraces and cliffs formed during lower sea levels have also been mapped on the South African shelf (Cawthra et al., 2014, 2015) and in detail at several sites on shelves around the Iberian Peninsula and the Balearic Islands (Fernández-Salas et al., 2015). These include a series of terraces on the Cantabrian Shelf at depths of 37 m, 52–56 m, 70–75 m and 87–90 m (Galparsoro et al., 2010); several terraces between 50 and 84 m on the Gulf of Cadiz Shelf; and terraces on the middle and outer areas of the southeastern and Balearic shelves (Fernández-Salas et al., 2015). source_locator.quote_or_paraphrase: Wave-cut terraces and cliffs formed during lower sea levels have also been mapped on the South African shelf (Cawthra et al., 2014, 2015) and in detail at several sites on shelves around the Iberian Peninsula and the Balearic Islands (Fernández-Salas et al., 2015). These include a series of terraces on the Cantabrian Shelf at depths of 37 m, 52–56 m, 70–75 m and 87–90 m (Galparsoro et al., 2010); several terraces between 50 and 84 m on the Gulf of Cadiz Shelf; and terraces on the middle and outer areas of the southeastern and Balearic shelves (Fernández-Salas et al., 2015). Heavy black lines—faults. Orange lines—active faults mapped in this study. Dashed black lines—marine terrace rollovers, numbered T1–T8. Yellow dashed line—distal edge of marine terrace sediments. Dashed orange line—approximate contact between uplifted and truncated Miocene sedimentary rocks (seaward) and igneous/metamorphic basement (shoreward) subcropping beneath terrace sediments. source_locator.quote_or_paraphrase: Submerged paleoshorelines and terraces surrounding Santa Catalina Island and the Pilgrim/Kidney Banks in the California Continental Borderland demonstrate late Quaternary tectonic subsidence, in contrast to the other islands of the California Continental Borderland that are experiencing tectonic uplift. We used high-resolution seismicreflection profiles to map a terrace package containing 16 successive parasequences surrounding Santa Catalina Island, preserved at depths from 30 to 470 m below modern mean sea level. We used a combination of multichannel seismic (MCS) and chirp subbottom profiles collected by Stanford University in 2014 and 2015 (Fig. 2, inset 2) to visually identify and navigate to key sedimentary horizons exposed on the walls of the submarine canyon. source_locator.quote_or_paraphrase: Douglas (1981) made a comprehensive study of benthic foraminifera in the California Continental Borderland and recognized eight recurrent benthic faunal assemblages characteristic of (1) offshore bank and terrace deposits with a mean upper water depth of 50 m, and (2) offshore ridge and deep bank assemblages with a mean upper water depth of 150 m. Five of the benthic species characterizing Douglas’ (1981) offshore bank and terrace assemblage, including Cassidulina limbata, Cassidulina subglobosa, Cassidulina tortuosa, Cibicides fletcheri, and Gavelinopsis (Rotorbinella) companulata, together comprise 48%–68% of the benthic assemblages identified in samples NA067-002 through NA067-020 (Fig. 5, profiles 2610, 2207). source_locator.quote_or_paraphrase: Terraces. Along the south coast, the 245 m isobath separates the inner shelf from the mid-shelf (Fig. 2). This bathymetric zone is distinct and forms a shelf terrace, or break in the dominant gradient. Heap & Harris (2008) defined terraces as relatively flat areas of regional extent, bordered by steeper margins. The marked change in gradient at this depth has been described by Martin & Flemming (1987) as one of four prominent south coast nickpoints (at water depths of 105–100, 80–75, 55– 50 and c. 40 m).

source_locator.quote_or_paraphrase: Sea cliffs. Offshore of Cape St Blaize, the hardrock geological substrate that characterizes the Pinnacle Point coastline extends offshore for 1.5 km towards the east (Fig. 4). From the base at 23 m bmsl the submerged seacliffs rise 20 m off the seabed and this outcrop correlates to Acoustic Facies a on the side-scan sonar record (Table 1). The seawards margin of the outcrop is defined by incised caves and overhangs, and is eroded into an embayment comparable in size and form to the presently exposed coastal cliffs at Pinnacle Point and Cape St Blaize. These submerged seacliffs are therefore a lowstand expression of a continuous coastal promontory.

source_locator.quote_or_paraphrase: Shelf banks/shoals. A shelf bank/shoal is considered to be an area of seabed characterized by widely spaced isobaths at the base, intercepted by areas of relief and incision (Heap & Harris 2008). Numerous ridges with prominent relief compared to the surrounding seabed lie in water depths of 35–55 m in the eastern side of the study area offshore the Groot Brak River (Fig. 5). The ridges are typically separated by depressions – hence, the classification as shoals – but in places they coalesce, bifurcate and they can be grouped into distinct clusters. The most significant linear features trend east–west and individual ridges reach maximum widths of 400 m. In the study area, the shoals extend 6.5 km along-strike and continue eastwards beyond the extent of the mapped zone. These features are cl

source_locator.quote_or_paraphrase: th of 55 m bmsl. The shelf within the mapped region can be subdivided into various bathymetric zones. The present Mossel Bay shoreline is flanked by a relatively wide (up to 8 km) inner continental shelf terminated by a terrace that defines the drop-off and start of the mid-shelf at a depth of 45 m below msl. The morphology is smooth in the two embayments of Vlees Bay in the west and Mossel Bay in the east (Fig. 1), but the inner shelf narrows in the region adjacent to the Pinnacle Point coastline (Figs 1 & 2). We delineate two distinct morphological zones of the mapped shelf that are separated by a terrace/break in slope. The raised beach (Fig. II. 6) rests partly against a former cliff and partly upon a former sea-bed of Chalk, and at its most south-westerly extent it is situated about 12 or 15 feet above the present high-water level. From that elevation it dips gradually in the direction of Sangatte, until it passes out of sight beneath the shingle of the existing shore. The large well-rounded flints which compose it are firmly cemented together in a chalky matrix, and blocks of this conglomerate are to be seen scattered on the beach. Clearly, if the older terraces are still horizontal over long distances, the younger ones cannot have been warped. In some cases it is noted that the younger, horizontal terraces truncate the undulating pattern of the older (see NAKANO, 1954). Yet another factor of instability can be seen in the great deltas, where compaction of water-saturated muds must inevitably play a great role; but how much and when is not yet clear. source_locator.quote_or_paraphrase: The occurrence of the base of the continental escarpment at a depth of 120–130 m substantiates that this feature potentially represents the shoreline of the Maltese archipelago during the LGM, when the parts of the continental shelf now located at a depth of ,130 m were emerged and affected by subaerial processes (Lambeck et al. 2011; Micallef et al. 2013). source_locator.quote_or_paraphrase: In the area offshore of NW Gozo, we observed a N–S-orientated escarpment that has a slope gradient of 208–358, and a depth ranging between 90 and 130 m. source_locator.quote_or_paraphrase: ak of slope at a depth of approximately 25– 50 m in the NW area of Malta changes orientation from W–E to NNW–SSE in front of Gebel Imbark. It is indented, has a slope gradient of 158 and is defined by a number of marine terraces. The base of the deeper marine terrace is located at a depth of around 130 m, while the shallower terraces occur at different depths (15, 49 and 76 m). These terraces are flat or sloping slightly seawards, and are bounded by a break of slope. They have been interpreted as palaeoshore platforms shaped by wave action during the post-glacial sea-level rise, being morphologically similar to modern shore platforms occurring along the coastline.

source_locator.quote_or_paraphrase: an average rate of 5 mm a21 (Lambeck et al. 2011). By 14.4 ka, the sea level had dropped to 2100 m. The land bridge had narrowed to less than 10 km and a good part of the shore platform associated with the 2130 m palaeoshoreline had been drowned. By 12.9 ka, the land bridge had become largely submerged and the Maltese archipelago became disconnected from Sicily; the archipelago at this time had an area of 720 km2 , which is more than twice the present surface area. The islands of Malta, Gozo and Comino were connected until 8.6 ka, when the Sikka l-Bajda limestone plateau became an island. Sea-level rise during the following few thousand years submerged the remaining 65 km2 of palaeolandscape to give rise to the current configuration of the Maltese Islands.
source_locator.section: Submergence of the Maltese palaeolandscape after the Last Glacial Maximum

source_locator.quote_or_paraphrase: A number of terraces and nickpoints representing low sea level stands have been observed in cchosounder and continuous seismic profiles at depths of 64 m, 90 m, 130 m, 170–175 m and at about 200 m. The first three of these terraces correspond closely to those reported on other parts of the continental shelf of Australia such as the Sahul Shelf $^{7}$ and the Great Barrier Reef $^{8}$ . A shallow-water fossiliferous algal rock recovered by dredge from a submerged reef between 130 m and 175 m (station 1231, Fig. 1) gave a radiocarbon date of $18,700 \pm 350$ yr BP (Table 1). source_locator.figure: Fig. 16. Illustration of MSLD 20 features using satellite-derived bathymetry and regional DEM from Lebrec et al. (2021).
source_locator.quote_or_paraphrase: Fig. 16. Illustration of MSLD 20 features using satellite-derived bathymetry and regional DEM from Lebrec et al. (2021). At least three groups of sub-parallel relict features can be identified at depth of 16 m (r2), 18 m (r3) and 24 m (r4). Locally in nearshore area ridges can be observed in 3 m of water depth (r1). Unconsolidated relict sediments are often reworked to form modern bedforms attached to the relict ridges (rw). Relict ridges are continuous between Dampier and Broome except near fluvial systems such as the De Grey Delta where they are overlaid by modern sediments (m). text_image images/c6094a70d6e70a6f634d0727fdcf06c493cb5ddd8d88971c3aabaf78e79c44b4.jpg

source_locator.quote_or_paraphrase: nd were observed offshore Dampier and Broome in water depth of 30 m and 125 m, respectively. Individual clinothems are 1 to 5 m thick and extends over 600 m (Fig. 10). Such morphologies correspond to the description of “shoreline clinoforms” (Patruno and Helland-Hansen, 2018), which encompasses delta front and beach shoreface deposits. Given that no major fluvial systems are identified in their direct vicinity, we interpret these features as beach shoreface strata accumulated by wave processes. This interpretation is supported by the clinoforms angles, < 0.1 degree, which are below the expected threshold of delta front mouth bars (Cosgrove et al., 2018; Paumard et al., 2019; Paumard et al., 2020) and by the presence of relict beach ridges landward of the clinothems.

source_locator.quote_or_paraphrase: Sub-horizontal terraces are observed from both the bathymetry and the 2D seismic data at depths of 17, 35 and 90 m. They are characterised by gentle seaward dipping surfaces and are backed by a terrace riser (Fig. 12B–E). Both criteria are distinctive morphologies of marine terraces formed through wave erosion (Anderson et al., 1999; Blanchon and Jones, 1995; Bradley, 1958). Similar features can be observed along the modern coast (Fig. 12E), confirming the nature of the terraces. source_locator.quote_or_paraphrase: This being said, a few comments can be made on the age and preservation of the Rowley Shelf MSLD shorelines. First, it should be noted that the LGM was identified at depths comprised between 121 and 125 m below sea level in the Bonaparte Basin (Sahul Shelf), further north along the NWS (Yokoyama et al., 2001a), which suggests that MSLD 125 could correspond to the LGM. This interpretation is further supported by age dating performed offshore Barrow Island, at IODP site U1461, that identified the LGM 14 m below the seabed, in water depth of 127 m (Ishiwa et al., 2019b), in line with the observation of MSLD 125 offshore Broome (Fig. 15D and B). source_locator.quote_or_paraphrase: ccumulate on the outer shelf, leaving relict features outcropping on most of the seabed (James et al., 2004; Jones, 1971; Jones, 1973). Interestingly, no kilometre-scale relict bioconstructed reefs, including coral-reef terraces, were observed along the Rowley Shelf during this study. This is in stark contrasts to the eastward facing Great Barrier Reef, along which relict linear reefs several km-long were identified at water depths of 80 m, 90 m, 100 m and 110 m (Webster et al., 2018). source_locator.quote_or_paraphrase: In that regard, the largest relict ebb-tidal deltas, characteristic of tidal environments, belong to MSLD 105 and MSLD 80 shorelines (Fig. 11F–G). They are associated with lagoons, filled by large accumulations of ooids and peloids, dated 15.7 to 12.4 ka BP (James et al., 2004), and extensive barriers, over 15 m high. Such accumulation of ooids and peloids were previously used to infer that the shelf was once bordered by a tide-influenced ‘Bahama-like’ platform (Dix et al., 2005; Gallagher et al., 2018), indicating a dry, sediment starved environment. source_locator.quote_or_paraphrase: merged relict coastal features spread evenly across the shelf from 5 to 145 m of water depth. Relict features of the Rowley Shelf belong to each of the categories identified from the review, apart from relict coral-reef terraces (i.e., coral reefs with a clear terrace morphology allowing location of palaeo sea level using geophysical data), which were not identified within the area of interest. Such a conclusion does not exclude the local presence of other types of drowned coral reefs that are out of the scope of this study. Relict features appear to be concentrated at specific water depths, which represent modal sea-level depths, at which the shoreline remained stable over longer periods of time. source_locator.quote_or_paraphrase: Fig. 4. Sea-level curves from the tectonically active region of the Caucasus (eastern Black Sea; A, B, C) and the passive margin of the northwest Black Sea (D ¼ near Dniester River; E ¼ off Crimea). Modified from (Pirrazoli, 1991, Plate 27: Black Sea II). Note that no large rivers flow into the Black Sea in the Caucasus and Crimea (see text for further discussion). Large black and small open arrows indicate mid-points of major and lesser ocean-atmosphere reorganizations, respectively, also discussed in text. Original sources for curves are: A ¼ Balabanov and Izmailov (1988); B ¼ Ostrovsky et al. (1977); C ¼ Tchepalyga (1984); D ¼ Voskoboinikov et al., 1982; E ¼ Nevessky (1970). line images/fd8529d0ab5d78d7daf873b549eeec6d2c4e79021091689d5c8f0c216cdc7cc3.jpg

source_locator.quote_or_paraphrase: The −80 m marine terrace should also be regarded as a marine-built terrace, as it is the surface expression of a sediment bank. However, its stratigraphic position, overlying an erosional surface and capped by the regressive facies of the most recent Carchuna IPW, would be compatible with a transgressive context. Therefore, we postulate that the −80 m depositional terrace is a postglacial feature. source_locator.quote_or_paraphrase: An absolute age can be assigned to all the terraces identified (Figs. 12 and 13). A basement marine-cut terrace at 110 m depth (110a) is the oldest and was formed at approximately 150 ka (Fig. 12). This terrace is only detected by gravity data (Figs. 7 and 8). The next recognizable terrace is the one located at 90 m depth and detected buried onshore (Figs. 7 and 8) as well as offshore (Figs. 9 to 11). This terrace formed at 65 ka during a regressive period (Fig. 12). Later on, in the next regressive period, the marine terrace at 100 m depth was formed at 50–35 ka. source_locator.quote_or_paraphrase: Four buried marine-cut terraces at 26, 48, 90 and 110 m below the current sea-level were detected using gravity data. They are integrated with depositional marine terraces offshore at 20, 80, 90 and 100; and with the shelf break at 110 m depths. The terrace located at 90 m water depth, which shows erosional features, is the only one identified both onshore and offshore. These data lead us to propose ages for the different marine terraces in view of different sea-level curves (Table 1 and Fig. 13). source_locator.figure: Fig. 3b: Raised beaches near Brora, Sutherland. The ‘beach and cliff 1’ is on the left (see Fig. 3a) looking north towar
source_locator.quote_or_paraphrase: Fig. 3b: Raised beaches near Brora, Sutherland. The ‘beach and cliff 1’ is on the left (see Fig. 3a) looking north towards Helmsdale and Navidale. The cliff has a clean sharp profile indicating its extreme youthfulness. This may be in part due to it having been exposed to wave action for a relatively short period during a retreat of the sea after c. AD 1200. The present beach is already forming a fledgling ‘clifflet’ of no more than 5m high that might have started as late as two to three hundred years ago. natural_image images/adbc913ada2d12c561d8a8e7ba5c2c3ecfa824b8a13a6ef9516eccf4a9150a6a.jpg

source_locator.figure: Fig. 8: A flood model of the Thames Valley. A chronology of maritime features unearthed by archaeologists compared to th
source_locator.quote_or_paraphrase: Fig. 8: A flood model of the Thames Valley. A chronology of maritime features unearthed by archaeologists compared to their altitude along the valley. If it was not for the recent discoveries of the early medieval Queenshithe, and the even more recent discovery of the Anglo-Saxon rubbish pits at Lake End, Dorney Wood, one might be excused for thinking that the tidal Thames in the present Port of London Authority district had stayed at more or less the same level since Roman times. Dorney Wood has the archaeologists puzzled because it defies normal explanations. However, it can easily be explained as a beach-market served by the ebb and flow of the tide. (a) The Seaward barrier between Sedgefield and Gerickespunt (Site 9) which attains an elevation of over 180 m amsl. (b) Sea-cliffs at Gerickespunt eroded into the Seaward barrier revealing a complex internal architecture of stacked parabolic dunes with the bedding preserved in a pristine condition by the lithification of the sand into aeolianite (Site 9a). (c) Aeolianite exposure in the Seaward barrier at Sedgefield (near Site 17) illustrating preserved high-angle bedding (base) and a rhizolithic rich zone (top) indicating former palaeosols or palaeo-surfaces. source_locator.quote_or_paraphrase: Evidence for higher sea levels is recognised throughout the Wilderness embayment. These include erosional “terraces” at various altitudes, e.g. the rock platform at w6e8 m amsl backed by a cliff the island in Eilandvlei, and exposures of marginal marine calcarenite typically ca 5e7 m above modern sea levels (Martin, 1962; Fig. 2f), e.g. at the base of the Seaward barrier to the east of Eilandvlei. Also, much of the seaward margin of the Middle barrier (e.g. between Sedgefield and the Goukamma River) has been oversteepened, forming a clear palaeo-sea cliff (Illenberger, 1996; Fig. 1b). This evidence indicates marine inundations of a significant portion of the embayment had taken

source_locator.quote_or_paraphrase: The Western Pacific is strewn with chains and clusters of seamounts, many of which are now flat-topped submarine seamounts, or guyots, with summit depths of about 1500 m. Many guyots are capped by shallow-water carbonate platform sediments overlying volcanic substrate. Guyot sediments can serve as "dip sticks" to monitor subsidence rates and relative changes in sea level for times when upward carbonate-platform growth occurred during tectonic subsidence of their foundations. Such sediments encode the timing and sense of sea-level rises and falls in their mineralogy, textures, and fossils. Guyot volcanic pedestals preserve, in their mineralogy and chemistry, clues to the nature of their parent mantl

source_locator.quote_or_paraphrase: The profiles demonstrate that all three guyots are capped with domes of sediment exceeding 100 m in maximum thickness, with slopes of 1–3 degrees. Beneath this capping is a ‘basement’ reflector below which no coherent seismic reflectors could be identified. The summit depths of these guyots range from 1540 to 1702 m, with heights above the surrounding ocean basin of 2900–3600 m, and side slopes between 15 and 25 degrees (Table 1).

source_locator.quote_or_paraphrase: The subsidiary shorelines that occur landward of the main barrier shoreline appear to be back-stepped remnants of smaller barriers that formed during transgression up to a depth of 28 m. These are likely to have formed after the initial slowstand that allowed the main barrier to form, and they mark a second, slow migration of the shoreline over the coastal profile at shallower depths, producing smaller, more poorly preserved barrier remnants.

source_locator.quote_or_paraphrase: Minimum summit depths along the Geisha guyots lie within a relatively narrow range, from 1647 m (Charlie Johnson) to 1318 m (Takuyō-Daini). The minimum depths do not appear to vary significantly either along the 1000-km-long chain or as a function of plateau area. The mean summit depth for the eight guyots is $1418 \pm 59$ m. This average does not differ significantly from a 1400-m average for the six guyots known to Heezen et al. [1973]. source_locator.quote_or_paraphrase: Slightly different stratigraphic successions are associated with the abrasion platforms in the nearby Sites 5 and 3 (Fig. 2). The paleoshoreline T2 remnant outcropping in Site 5 (Fig. 5), well exposed in a N-S oriented quarry wall, consists of a horizontal wave-cut platform located at 115 m a.s.l. covered by about 3 m thick cross-bedded, moderately sorted, fine arenites (Sample 5a, Supplementary materials 2) that, in the inner part of the abrasion platform, pass laterally into a coarsegrained massive deposit (Fig. 5A).

source_locator.quote_or_paraphrase: Intense quarrying activity has been carried out in the eastern part of Mt. Fellino ridge (Sites 6 and 7; Fig. 2), where only few outcrops of the raised paleoshorelines are available. Of those quarries, the huge one in Site 6 corresponds to one of the outcrops detected by Romano et al. (1994). There, an abrasion platform (Fig. 7A–D), which cuts the limestone strata dipping at high angle towards the S-SE, is covered by a thin veneer of clast-supported, well-rounded carbonate pebble and cobble which pass upwards to a decimetre-thick layer of arenites with quartz grains (Fig. 7B–C). The elevation of the abrasion platform inner edge is located at 110 m a.s.l. (Fig. 7).

source_locator.quote_or_paraphrase: al places. It is highly likely that sections of the shelf have failed and moved downslope, with material being deposited in the Santa Monica and Santa Cruz basins, modifying or destroying sections of the oldest lowstand terraces. In comparison, the slopes below the shelf break on the northern sides of the islands are gentler and display little evidence of gully formation or slope failure, possibly a result of the higher rate of deposition and greater volume of sediment being deposited in the Santa Barbara basin.

(sample D6359–2) shells from this area may be due to the more extensive habitat range of these particular fauna rather than being part of a submerged lowstand shoreline faunal assemblage. An age of 16,551 ± 379 yr B.P. was obtained from an H. kennerleyi shell (sample D6373–1) collected at a depth of \~110 m on the isolated ridge separated from the shelf by the Santa Cruz Channel south of Gull Island, with another post-LGM age of $1 6 , 5 5 3 \pm 4 1 3$ yr B.P. from a subtidal T. nuttallii shell (sample D6179–3) from \~90 m on the northern shelf edge of the Northern Channel Islands platform (Fig. 5).

source_locator.quote_or_paraphrase: slope break is found at a depth between 120 and 130 m, with the deeper one at \~140 m. The equivalent of the shelf edge surrounding the Northern Cannel Islands platform, which forms the most seaward extent of the lower terrace level, is at \~160–170 m around the northern and central bank highs. A less well defi ned abrasion platform, present around the pinnacle of the central high, is at \~100–105 m (Fig. 8A). During dives on the central and northern highs, notched and undercut cliff faces and well-rounded pebblethrough boulder-sized rocks were observed, most notably associated with abrasion and shell-dominated depositional platforms at \~120 m.

source_locator.quote_or_paraphrase: ene submergence rate of 3.03 ± 0.03 mm/ yr. On the other hand, a ca. 18 ka H. kennerleyi sample collected at 124 m (group 4a in Fig. 9) on the northern outcrop at Pilgrim Banks and ca. 26 ka samples collected on a lower terrace level at 142 m (group 4b in Fig. 9), currently are at depths close to the predicted sea level for their ages. This mismatch in the age-depth relationship can be interpreted in two ways—either as the result of a change in the rate, polarity, and magnitude of vertical change of the ridge before and after the Younger Dryas, or as a refl ection of organism growth and postmortem depositional mechanism tied to the rise in sea level.

source_locator.quote_or_paraphrase: In 1975, four bore holes were drilled into fringing reefs (“shoreface terraces”) rising to within 0.1 m of modern low-tide level on the flanks of Toagel Channel (Figure 2) between Koror and Babeldaob as part of the engineering investigations for building an interisland bridge. All the bore holes penetrated the full thickness of Holocene limestone resting unconformably, at depths of 21–26 m below mean sea level, on varicolored paleosol or colluvium of presumed Pleistocene age that is 3–4 m thick above volcanic basement described as fractured basaltic andesite (Easton and Ku 1980). source_locator.quote_or_paraphrase: A total of 32 (D1 to D32) superficial outcrop of FSST with 2200 m width were identified in Darango Bay (Fig. 4). The first superficial outcrop of a paleoshoreline is located at 14.6 m elevation and 2200 m away from the present shoreline. An old estuary 188 m wide, separated from the sea by new coastal ridges, was identified. Old tidal channels with 2 m depth were detected between the D30 and D27 paleoshoreline. Surveying of paleoshorelines showed that D22 and D31 are well-preserved geoindicators for dating and evaluation of coastal progradation. Therefore, ages of the paleoshorelines were calculated to be 1793 ± 46 Cal BP and 633 ± 45 Cal BP based on radiocarbon dating of collected articulate fossils.

source_locator.quote_or_paraphrase: Chabahar bay, previously studied by Gharibreza and Motamed (2006), shows 21 paleoshorelines, with the oldest located at 15 m above the present shoreline. These fossil beaches are superficial outcrops of FSST and steps of coastal progradation arranged over 5300 m width. Specific species of undisturbed articulate marine bivalve mollusks in Chabahar Bay were Cardina, Tellina, Mercenaria, Anodontia, Arca, and Anadara. Several species of gastropods such as Bursa, Architectonica, Oliva, Bula, Cyprea, Polinicess, and Murex were identified in the Chabahar Bay. source_locator.quote_or_paraphrase: Evidence of submerged paleoshorelines on tectonically stable continental shelves is represented by relict coastal depositional features that formed during periods of lower sea level. This study investigates two paleoshoreline features that extend 70 km from the Barwon Bank along the southeast Fraser Shelf, off eastern Australia. They are defined by well-lithified, mixed carbonate-siliciclastic deposits now at water depths of 60 m and between 80 and 100 m, located approximately 40 km seaward of the modern shoreline. source_locator.figure: Fig. 1. A. Map of Lake Ontario basin showing lowstand and highstand post-glacial lake shorelines. IR-Lake Iroquois (w12.
source_locator.quote_or_paraphrase: Fig. 1. A. Map of Lake Ontario basin showing lowstand and highstand post-glacial lake shorelines. IR-Lake Iroquois (w12.5 ka BP); AM-Lake Admiralty (w11.7e11.4 ka BP); SH-Lake Stanley-Hough (w8e9 ka BP); AL-Lake Algonquin (11.2e10.4 ka BP). Location of known terrestrial Paleoindian sites is also shown. During Admiralty lowstand more than 10,000 km2 of the present lakebed was exposed and available for prehistoric human occupation. Inset shows map of North America and location of Lake Ontario. B. Digital elevation model (DEM) for Rice Lake showing modern shoreline with locations of known prehistoric (Paleoindian-Woodland age) archaeological sites (based on 1:50,000 NTS digital topographic data). Sites within Rice Lake are terrestrial sites located on small islands. text_image images/86a8b72e

source_locator.quote_or_paraphrase: 12 ka BP (Sonnenburg, 2010). The transgression of the wetland shoreline can be seen clearly in both the open lake and in the basin cores in the declining age of the peat deposits at higher elevations. In the open lake for example, the peat had an age of 9530  50 bp (11,091e10,682 cal. BP) at 7.2 m bpl (RIL-11) and further up basin slope an ages of 9130 50 bp (10,478e10,204 cal. BP) at a depth of 5.4 m bpl (RIL-8) (Figs. 2 and 3). A similar trend was observed in the McIntyre lagoon, where the shoreline transgressed 1 m between 9470 50 bp (11,069e10,574 cal. BP) (RIL-10) and 8760 50 bp (10,114e9555 cal. BP) (RIL-16) (Fig. 2).

source_locator.quote_or_paraphrase: Fig. 23. StrataboxTM sub-bottom profiles of shoreface transects near Tuktoyaktuk: (a) in vicinity of starved transgressive barrier to the southwest (b– b′ in Fig. 20); (b) off Tuktoyaktuk Island $( \mathsf { c } - \mathsf { c ^ { \prime } }$ in Fig. 20). To the SW (a), the TU is at the seabed in depths less than 4 m, but buried by deltaic-marine mud to a depth of about 4 m further seaward; note 2 m terrace and interpreted occurrence of submarine permafrost (pf). Off Tuktoyaktuk Island, the nearshore slope is very steep, dropping into a submerged thermokarst lake basin, the fill of which has not been truncated by the TU. properties.description: submerged beach at The transgressive unconformity and subsequent erosion and sedimentation, reported around 20 m depth
properties.indicator_subtype: narrative fallback: submerged geomorphic sea-level feature
properties.location_name: The transgressive unconformity and subsequent erosion and sedimentation
properties.notes: Narrative fallback cluster from prose description of submerged palaeolandscape; depth uncertainty widened pending human review.
properties.site_name: The transgressive unconformity and subsequent erosion and sedimentation

source_locator.quote_or_paraphrase: and inundated-valley estuaries. Wave quarrying of pits in exposed estuarine sediments at depths of 20 m or more confirms the ongoing deepening of the transgressive unconformity as much as 3 km seaward of the present-day shoreline and 6000 years after the shoreline migrated landward past the point of erosion (Figs 12 & 25a). Pit formation of this kind has not been widely described in the literature, but is known from the Atlantic coast of Nova Scotia (Forbes et al. 1991b). There, as in this study, wave quarrying of cohesive sediments is assumed to begin where the TU becomes exposed at the seabed. Although ice scour penetrating through the TU could be a mechanism for initiation, the depth at which these features are currently encountered would seem to eliminate ice as a plausible trigger.

source_locator.figure: Fig. 9. Schematic interpretation of kinematics and uplift patterns for the Central Hellenic orogenic wedge, with individ
source_locator.quote_or_paraphrase: Fig. 9. Schematic interpretation of kinematics and uplift patterns for the Central Hellenic orogenic wedge, with individual interpretations of: (a) Western Crete and (b) Eastern Crete. In both sections, frontal accretion of sediments takes place in the vicinity of the Mediterranean Ridge; To the north, under the island of Crete, structural underplating of sediment leads to long wavelength, secular uplift. The uplift signal is localized by the geometry of the upper plate with underplating and uplift confined to trenchward and around the of the contact between the upper plate Moho and the top of the lower plate. The interior of the upper plate is extending in the Cretan Sea, and crustal thinning and subsidence affect parts of the north coast of Crete. text_image images/0caa64f031c236909fa059

source_locator.quote_or_paraphrase: the predicted littoral sand sink in the Grays Harbor basin could be filled by littoral sand that currently exists in adjacent barrier beaches. If all of the littoral sand that fills the estuary sand sink is supplied by shoreline retreat of the adjacent barriers (20 km in length), and assuming average vertical beach profile depths of 5–10 m, then net shoreward erosion distances of 500–1000 m of the current shoreline could occur in the study area from a potential 1.0 m submergence of the Grays Harbor basin.

source_locator.quote_or_paraphrase: # MARINE TRANSGRESSION, SHORELINE EMERGENCE: # EVIDENCE IN SEABED AND TERRESTRIAL GROUND TEMPERATURES # OF CHANGING RELATIVE SEA LEVELS, ARCTIC CANADA Alan E. Taylor Terrain Sciences Division, Geological Survey of Canada, Ottawa, Ontario Abstract. Precise temperatures to depths of several hundred meters have been measured in abandoned petroleum exploration wells in coastal and offshore areas of the Canadian Arctic. In these regions, changes in relative sea level during the Quaternary have left a strong thermal signature on deep ground temperatures. Surface temperature changes of the order of 10-20 K accompanied shoreline emergence due to uplift in some regions and marine transgression due to eustatic changes in sea levels in others. source_locator.quote_or_paraphrase: of 0.70907 which is substantially more radiogenic than the composition of the Black Sea lake water at that time (0.70890). This discrepancy is ascribed to colonization of perched ponds at elevations high above the paleoshoreline of the Black Sea-Lake (Major et al., 2006). Scherbakov and Babak (1979) describe the same faunal assemblage in sediments from the Sea of Azov which they also interpret to be perched saline ponds during periods of lowered Black Sea level.

source_locator.quote_or_paraphrase: eholes were drilled (AS6, AS7 and AS18 in Fig. 6) in the mudflat areas, which were partially occupied by inactive salt works. The low-lying (≈0 m asl) mudflats were bordered by outcrops from the post Barreiras Formation terraces and the Oligocene basalt outcrops. The XRD mineralogical analysis of sediments from borehole AS18 at depths of 0.35 m, 0.80 m and 2.2 m revealed illite dominant composition (up to 60%) with kaolinite (up to 15%), detritic feldspar and biotite. We collected organic matter samples from borehole AS6 at the 150–155 cm interval, which yielded an age of 7210 cal yr BP (Table 2, Fig. 2). The foraminifera data, combined with the clayey/silty and the organic detritus-rich nature of the sediment, indicated a mangrove environment.

source_locator.quote_or_paraphrase: eholes were drilled (AS6, AS7 and AS18 in Fig. 6) in the mudflat areas, which were partially occupied by inactive salt works. The low-lying (≈0 m asl) mudflats were bordered by outcrops from the post Barreiras Formation terraces and the Oligocene basalt outcrops. The XRD mineralogical analysis of sediments from borehole AS18 at depths of 0.35 m, 0.80 m and 2.2 m revealed illite dominant composition (up to 60%) with kaolinite (up to 15%), detritic feldspar and biotite. We collected organic matter samples from borehole AS6 at the 150–155 cm interval, which yielded an age of 7210 cal yr BP (Table 2, Fig. 2). The foraminifera data, combined with the clayey/silty and the organic detritus-rich nature of the sediment, indicated a mangrove environment.

source_locator.quote_or_paraphrase: Fig. 2. Isopach map of Holocene fill above the transgressive ravinement surface offshore of the CRLC barriers and beach plains. Fill thickness is shown in 10 m contour intervals, i.e., 10, 20, 30 and 40 m. The shelf isopach contours also provide a very approximate depth to the ravinement surface at the modern shorelines (see Twichell et al., 2010-this issue for details). Predicted ravinement surfaces under the modern beaches reach 30 m depth in the Clatsop subcell, 20 m depth in the Long Beach subcell, 10 m depth in the Grayland subcell, and only a few meters in depth in the North Beaches subcell, north of Grays Harbor. Bedrock is currently exposed in a shallow shoal area located in the inner-shelf offshore of Grays Harbor. text_image images/5caca4b092bfc5ec35cbb4135b729380e58b5cff68f18e28

source_locator.quote_or_paraphrase: shallow side of the 74 to 82 m isobath band of RLM, and have consistent base depths averaging 73 m. Some have morphologies similar to barrier islands or longshore bars, which are typically sand bodies deposited near the shoreline and within a few meters of SL (Penland and others 1988). Indeed, the location of the ridges along the landward side of the 74 to 82 m feature band is just what would be expected had they formed contemporaneously, the ridges at the shoreline and the RLM in shallow water offshore. source_locator.quote_or_paraphrase: to southern Brazilian between the climatic optimum of the MIS5e and the early Holocene. Corrêa (1996) proposed a sealevel curve between the Last Glacial Maximum and the early Holocene, based on the position of abrasion terraces on the southern Brazilian continental shelf and comparing them with global sea-level curves. Vicalvi et al. (1978) recognized a paleo-lagoon at the Abrolhos Depression (Eastern Brazilian shelf ), dating them at 10,600 yr BP (uncalibrated). Finally, Mahiques et al. (2011), presented four datings on different material from a beach-rock located at 13 mbsl São Sebastião (southeastern Brazil), which gave calibrated ages around 8,200 yr BP.
